blocks.text: He overheard their conversation and inquired into their purposes, and learned that they were preparing to lay hands on King Artaxerxes; and he informed the king concerning them.
Then the king examined the two eunuchs, and after they had confessed it, they were led away to execution.
The king made a permanent record of these things, and Mordecai wrote an account of them.
And the king ordered Mordecai to serve in the court, and rewarded him for these things.
5 : The king Mordecai immediately. This conflicts with 6.1–3, and suggests that this section is a secondary addition.
But Haman son of Hammedatha, a Bougean, who was in great honor with the king, determined to injure Mordecai and his people because of the two eunuchs of the king. END OF ADDITION A
12.1–6: Mordecai saves the king's life. It is unclear whether this conspiracy is the same as the one in Esth 2.19–23 of the Heb text or an earlier intrigue.

6 : The Gk text suggests (in contrast to the Heb) that Haman took part in the eunuchs’ foiled plot and thus provides a motivation for Mordecai's and Haman's subsequent actions. (see also 3.1) likely reflecting the Heb word “Agagite,” which connects Haman to the Amalekite king Agag, Saul's vanquished enemy (see 1 Sam 15.8). Mordecai and Haman share an ancestral enmity (Ex 17.8–16; Num 24.7).
